text: WHO recommends that free sugars make up less than 10% of total daily energy intake, and ideally under 5% (about 25 g) for additional health benefits. High-sugar packaged foods like soft drinks, biscuits and chocolate contribute to weight gain and dental caries.

WHO recommends less than 2 g of sodium (about 5 g of salt) per day for adults. Namkeen, chips, instant noodles and many sauces are high in sodium; reducing intake lowers blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

Saturated fat should be under 10% of total energy intake; replacing it with unsaturated fats from nuts, seeds and vegetable oils reduces cardiovascular disease risk. Fried snacks and many biscuits are high in saturated fat.

FSSAI and dietary guidelines for Indians recommend adequate dietary fibre (around 30 g/day) from whole grains, pulses, fruits and vegetables. Whole-grain breads, oats and millet-based products are higher in fibre than refined-flour (maida) products.
Protein-rich options such as roasted chana, peanuts, milk, curd, paneer and pulses make more filling snacks than refined-carbohydrate snacks. Look for products with higher protein per 100 g.

Healthier swaps for sugary soft drinks include water, unsweetened nimbu pani, buttermilk (chaas), or 100% fruit juice in small portions. Diluted or no-added-sugar options cut free-sugar intake substantially.
Instead of fried chips and namkeen, choose baked snacks, roasted makhana, roasted chana or plain popcorn to reduce fat and sodium while keeping the crunch.
Digestive, oat or multigrain biscuits generally have more fibre and less sugar than cream-filled or glucose biscuits. Checking the sugar per 100 g helps pick a better option.
Plain oats, muesli without added sugar, and millet flakes are lower in added sugar than frosted or chocolate cereals. Add fruit for natural sweetness instead of sugar.
When comparing packaged foods, read the per-100 g column (not per-serving) so products are comparable. Prioritise lower sugar, lower sodium and lower saturated fat, and higher fibre and protein.
